risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A computer program generated a random number sequence, which was used to allocate subjects to the treatment or wait-list control group." Comment: This explicitly states that allocation was based on a computer-generated random number sequence, which is a truly random method. The use of permuted blocks does not reduce confidence because the random generation method is described.

Quote: "Randomisation to one of two study arms is done by sealed envelope method... The randomisation schedule and sealed envelopes were prepared by an individual who worked closely with the Centre for Statistical Design and Analysis and was not directly involved in the study." Comment: The report explicitly states a sealed-envelope allocation method, with the envelopes and schedule prepared by someone not involved in the study. Although it does not spell out every SNOSE feature (opaque, sequentially numbered), the described procedure is the only clear concealment mechanism reported and indicates the recruiter could not foresee assignments.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Blinding is not possible for the HARMONY Study due to the wait-list control design of the study. However, those delivering the MBSR therapy are not informed as to which arm subjects are randomised to." Comment: Participants were not blinded because the trial used an obvious wait-list control, and although therapists were blinded to allocation, participants knew whether they were receiving immediate MBSR or waiting. Because the main outcomes were ambulatory and office blood pressure, participant knowledge could plausibly influence behaviour (e.g., lifestyle changes, adherence, reporting) and thus the outcomes.

Quote: "Blinding is not possible for the HARMONY Study due to the wait-list control design of the study. However, those delivering the MBSR therapy are not informed as to which arm subjects are randomised to." Comment: Outcome assessment for the primary outcome was based on ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (ABPM), which is an automated measurement system: "Participants wear an ambulatory blood pressure monitor... Blood pressure is recorded every 15 min during the day and every 30 min between 23:00 and 7:00. Mean values calculated for systolic and diastolic blood pressure." Because the primary outcome is objectively recorded by the device rather than by a subjective assessor judgement, lack of assessor blinding is unlikely to influence measurement of the outcome.

Quote: "The primary outcome will be examined using an intent-to-treat analysis. An intent-to-treat population is defined as all subjects in the study who completed at least one session of MBSR." Comment: The report indicates ITT analysis and the study appears to have only modest, manageable attrition: 50 randomized to intervention and 51 to comparison, with 3 control participants starting antihypertensive medication and their ambulatory BP censored from that point onward. There is no clear signal of substantial or imbalanced dropout for the primary 12-week outcome, so attrition is unlikely to have biased the result.
